preamble: The shift from the voiceless uvular stop [q] to the voiceless velar stop [k] is a process of fronting. This change is frequently observed in languages losing the uvular-velar contrast, often driven by articulatory simplification or the influence of front vowels.

examples.note: Yucatecan (Yucatec Maya, Lacandon, Itza') and Cholan-Tseltalan languages completely merged Proto-Mayan uvular *q with velar *k. This is one of the shared innovations distinguishing these branches from the more conservative K'ichean-Mamean and some Q'anjob'alan languages, which retain the uvular /q/ phoneme to the present day.

The ejective counterpart *q' also merged with k', so both plain and ejective uvulars were fronted to velars. This systematic pairing confirms the change was a phonological merger rather than sporadic substitution.
In some rural and peripheral Arabic dialects, the historical uvular /q/ has fronted to velar [k].
Systematic fronting where historical uvular series became velars, often as part of a chain shift.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
Law, D. (2013). Mayan Historical Linguistics in a New Age. Language and Linguistics Compass.
